The combination of position advantage, Minni's desperation (3,355 chips), and my large stack (22,200) makes this an aggressive play designed to win the pot immediately or outflop weak holdings in a favorable position.

I am holding 2h 4h in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the Button, and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given the strength indicated by a 3-bet from the chip leader and my extremely weak hand, there is no reason to commit more chips to this pot. Folding is the most prudent action.

I opened to 300 on the button with J5o to take the initiative, but Claude 3-bet to 800. Calling 500 into a 1200 pot (1700 after my call) requires roughly 29% equity. J5o is easily dominated by a typical 3-bet range and has poor post‑flop playability, so its equity is well below what's needed. 4‑bet bluffing is too risky here — he has deep stacks and can apply heavy pressure. I fold to avoid playing a bloated pot with a marginal hand and preserve my ~33BB stack for better spots.

J4o is a garbage hand with minimal equity post-flop. Despite being on the button and having a cheap call available, this hand lacks the connectivity and strength to play profitably. With a healthy stack of 22,650, I can afford to wait for better opportunities rather than gamble with weak hands. The positional advantage doesn't overcome the fundamental weakness of J4 offsuit. Folding preserves chips for stronger spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 3s, which is a very weak hand. Claude has already folded on the button. While it only costs 50 more to call, 53o has very little equity and poor playability out of position against Minni, who is currently the short stack. Given my goal is to maximize my stack over 150 hands, I will take the conservative route and fold this junk hand to avoid unnecessary losses.

In 3-handed play, the button has a very wide opening range. T8o is a marginal but acceptable hand to open-raise to steal the blinds or play in position. With Minni being relatively short-stacked and Claude being the chip leader, a standard raise puts pressure on the small blind while keeping the pot manageable against the big stack.

I’m in the small blind with Kc2s facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it’s 250 more to call (total committed would be 300). My stack is ~33bb (3355). Flash (button) open-raises a lot from the button and Claude (BB) routinely defends and barrels post‑flop — calling here often creates a multiway pot where I’ll be out of position against two aggressive, deep stacks. K2o is frequently dominated by button’s range (better Kx, Ax, broadways) and has poor playability OOP. 3‑betting light is too risky here against two big stacks and a lot of post‑flop aggression. Given the marginal equity vs likely ranges and the positional disadvantage, I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

Calling with A9o from the big blind against Flash's button raise. The hand has solid equity potential, the price is reasonable at only 200 more to call, and I'll have positional advantage postflop in this heads-up situation. Flash's raise from the button suggests a wide range that my ace-high can dominate or out-draw.

Checking with A9o on a dry paired flop (3-3-Q) while out of position. Ace-high likely has reasonable equity against Flash's button-raising range, but this board texture (paired, relatively static) and my positional disadvantage make checking the right play. This allows me to see Flash's action, control the pot size, and potentially improve on the turn while keeping flexibility. A9o is strong enough to continue but not strong enough to bet into Flash's wide range when I'm out of position.

On a paired flop of Q-3-3, as the pre-flop aggressor, I have a significant range advantage. Claude checked to me, which is expected. I will fire a continuation bet to represent a Queen or a strong pocket pair. Since the board is relatively dry (aside from the spade draw), a bet of around 40-50% of the pot is standard to fold out his air and marginal hands that missed. My 10-high has no showdown value, so I need to win the pot now or on later streets via aggression.

Calling with A9o on a paired 3-3-Q flop. The hand has solid equity against Flash's button-raising range, the pot odds are favorable at 4.17:1, and I need to balance my checking range by continuing with reasonable hands. A9o is strong enough to continue, and calling allows me to see the turn and make informed decisions based on new information while out of position. Folding too often to flop bets would be exploitable by an aggressive opponent like Flash.

Checking on the turn with A9o against Flash's weak turn check. Flash showed weakness by checking after raising pre-flop and betting the flop, suggesting their hand isn't strong. Ace-high has decent equity and I'm out of position, making seeing the river for free the optimal play. Checking maintains flexibility and allows me to make better decisions on the river based on the complete board and Flash's action.

On the turn 8d, I have improved to a pair of eights. While this gives me some showdown value, the board is still headlined by the Queen and the pair of threes. Claude checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. If I bet here, I'm rarely getting called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised by a Queen or a slow-played three. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow myself to realize my showdown value on the river, or potentially pick up a bluff if the board remains safe.

Flash's turn check on a dry paired board is a clear weakness indicator after betting the flop. My ace-high should be the strongest hand in most scenarios. Checking allows me to go to showdown and win the pot. Betting risks giving Flash a fold/call decision where they can fold weak hands or make a difficult decision that favors their continuing range. The simple, straightforward play is to check and win the pot with ace-high against Flash's weak range.

On the river 7s, the board now shows a potential spade flush. Claude has checked to me for the third time (counting the turn). My pair of eights has decent showdown value in this spot, especially since the board is paired with threes. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by any hand weaker than an eight, and I might get check-raised by a Queen, a slow-played three, or a back-door flush. Since Claude checked, I will check back to realize my showdown value and avoid losing more chips to a superior hand that is trapping or just playing cautiously.

With 6♦8♥ on the button in a 3-handed game (≈33bb effective) I should be opening a fairly wide range and take the initiative. Limping (calling 100) surrenders fold equity and invites aggressive action from the big/small blinds; a min-raise to 200 is too small and can be exploited. I’ll raise to a standard 3xBB (300) to seize the lead, keep the pot manageable, and play the flop in position. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold this marginal offsuit hand; I can continue only against small defenses or favorable flops.

Raising to 700 with pocket sixes. Minni is critically short-stacked (3055 chips) after his 300 raise, making him vulnerable. A raise here applies pressure to both opponents, builds value for my pair, and allows me to control the action. This also prevents Flash from making cheaper calls and taking unfavorable odds in a multiway pot. The raise size of ~2.3x his initial bet is standard and demonstrates strength without being recklessly aggressive given my 22,000 stack.

I am holding King-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni opened from the button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. K3o is a very weak hand and does not play well against a 3-bet range, especially being out of position. Given the action, I should comfortably fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I opened the button to 300 and Claude (SB) 3‑bet to 700. I have 6♦8♥ offsuit and ~30bb effective (stack 3055). Calling 400 (4bb, ~13% of my stack) to play a marginal offsuit hand out of position postflop vs a 3‑betting range is unattractive — this hand is often dominated and has poor equity versus a typical 3‑bet range. A thin 4‑bet to 1100 would bloat the pot with a weak holding and is unnecessarily risky. My preflop plan was to fold these marginal offsuit hands to a significant 3‑bet, so I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot in position.

I'm on the button with K♦8♠, an excellent position with a moderately playable hand. The preflop blinds total 150 chips. 

Key considerations:
1. **Position advantage**: Button is the strongest position, allowing me to act last preflop and first postflop
2. **Opponent stacks**: Minni (BB) has only 2,955 chips - critically short and likely playing tight or shoving light. Flash (SB) has 14,445 - decent but not massive
3. **Hand strength**: K8o is playable from the button, especially against short stacks. Kings play well, and position compensates for the weak kicker
4. **Stack management**: With 22,450, I'm comfortable raising and folding to aggression if needed, but I'm strong enough to defend my equity
5. **Standard opener**: A 3x BB raise (300) is standard from the button and establishes aggressive control of the pot

I'll raise to 300. This likely folds the weak short-stack or forces a shoving decision, allows me to take the blinds if unchallenged, or sets up a favorable position if Flash calls. Given Minni's critical chip count, they may be forced to shove with many hands, and K8 is ahead of a wide range of desperate holdings (any two cards hands that are dominated).

I have a pocket pair of sixes (6c, 6d) in the Small Blind. Claude, the button, has raised to 300. Minni, in the Big Blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 2955 chips. While 66 is a strong hand, especially three-handed, I am out of position against Claude, who has a larger stack. A call is standard here to set mine, but given Minni's short stack, she might shove. However, if I call, I am getting good odds. Raising is also an option to take control, but a call allows me to see a flop and evaluate. Given the tournament context and stack sizes, calling is a solid, low-variance play that keeps the pot manageable while still having post-flop potential.

Claude opened to 300 on the button and Flash (SB) has already called, making this a multiway pot. I hold K♣4♥ — a weak, easily dominated kicker and poor playability out of position. The pot is 700 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need roughly 22% equity (200/(700+200) ≈ 22.2%) to justify a call. Versus a button open and an active SB cold-call, K4o rarely has that equity and will often be dominated postflop. With ~29.5 BB effective (stack 2955) I should conserve chips and avoid marginal, multiway, OOP spots. A thin 3-bet/bluff isn’t attractive since the SB is in; fold and wait for a better situation.

I have a pocket pair of sixes on a relatively low and dry flop (8d-5c-2s). I am currently an overpair to the 5 and the 2, but there is one overcard (the 8) on the board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Claude, the standard play is to check to him and see how he proceeds. This board likely didn't hit his range particularly hard unless he has a pocket pair higher than mine or an 8. By checking, I can gauge the strength of his hand based on his betting action while keeping the pot manageable.

Betting 450 on the flop with K8 against Flash's check. The 8d5c2s board is dry and favorable to my middle pair with a king kicker. Flash's preflop call followed by a check indicates weakness. I'm in position with a strong stack, so betting for value here is standard. The bet sizes well relative to the pot (roughly 2/3 pot), which is proper value bet sizing. This likely wins the pot outright or allows me to continue with confidence on the turn if Flash calls.

On this dry 8-5-2 board, my pocket sixes are an overpair to the second and third cards but are beat by any 8 or higher pocket pair. Claude's pre-flop button raise range is wide and includes many hands I currently beat, such as overcards (AK, AQ, etc.). His c-bet of 450 is standard. Folding is too weak given my hand strength on this specific board, and raising would likely only get called by hands that have me beaten or have significant equity. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable, keep his bluffs in his range, and see how the turn card affects the board texture.

The Ten of Clubs on the turn is a problematic card for my pocket sixes as it is a second overcard to my pair. Claude, who raised from the button, could easily have a Ten in his range (AT, KT, QT, JT, or even T9). By checking, I am keeping the pot manageable and playing a 'pot control' strategy. I am out of position and don't want to build a large pot with a hand that is likely now just a bluff catcher. If Claude checks behind, I get to see a free river. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing to determine if I have enough equity to continue.

Betting 700 on the turn with top pair (K♦8♠ on 8♦5♣2♠T♣). Flash checked twice, indicating weakness. This is a value bet designed to extract chips from weaker pairs and unpaired hands. The 10 on the turn doesn't help Flash based on their checking pattern. Building the pot while in position with a strong hand is standard poker strategy here.

The Ten of Clubs on the turn is a second overcard to my pocket sixes, making my hand significantly weaker. However, Claude's bet sizing of 700 into a 1600 pot (roughly 44% of the pot) is small enough that I am getting decent pot odds (about 3:1) to continue. Given Claude's wide button opening range, he could still be double-barreling with overcards like AK, AQ, or KQ, or even a lower pair or a gutshot draw. I am currently in a 'bluff-catching' mode. Folding now would be too exploitable given the board texture and the price offered. I will call this bet to see the river, but I intend to check-fold to most large bets on the river unless the board improves my hand.

On the river, the board is 8d-5c-2s-Tc-Qc. My pocket pair of sixes has been weakened by three overcards (8, Ten, and Queen). I have called Claude's bets on the flop and turn, which indicates I have a marginal piece of the board. The Queen of Clubs on the river is another overcard that likely hits a significant portion of Claude's button-opening range (hands like AQ, KQ, QJ, or even back-door club flushes). I am out of position and my hand has devolved into a pure bluff-catcher. I will check to Claude to see if he checks behind with missed high cards like AK or AJ, or if he bets. If he bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard and most prudent play.

Betting top pair (K8 on 8d5c2s-Tc-Qc) on the river is standard after Flash has checked twice already. Flash's repeated checking indicates weakness and likely a marginal or missed hand. My stack of 21000 is healthy, and extracting value from weaker holdings is +EV. A 1000 chip bet (roughly 1/3 pot) is properly sized to induce calls from worse hands while maintaining solid risk management. This is profitable poker - converting a strong hand into chips against a passive opponent.

On the river, the board texture is 8d-5c-2s-Tc-Qc. My pocket pair of sixes is now an underpair to three cards on the board (8, Ten, and Queen). Claude, who raised from the button and has bet all three streets, is representing a strong range. While his bet sizing of 1000 into a 3000 pot (offering me 4:1 odds) is relatively small, the Queen is a significant scare card that likely hit many of the overcards in his opening range (AQ, KQ, QJ). My hand has devolved into a very weak bluff-catcher, and I lose to any 8, any Ten, any Queen, and any pocket pair higher than sixes (which are numerous in his range). Given the action and board development, it is highly probable that Claude has a superior hand. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with QsJs, which is a very strong premium hand in 3-handed play. With Minni being short-stacked (2905 chips) and Claude being the chip leader, I want to apply pressure and build a pot while in position. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to either take the blinds or set up a post-flop advantage.

I’m on the small blind with 7♣4♦ — a very weak, offsuit hand with poor post‑flop playability. Button opened to 300 (pot 450, I must call 250) and the big blind is still to act, so I’ll be out of position at best and likely face aggression. My stack is 2,905 (~29 BB), so a shove/3‑bet is unattractive against much deeper stacks and calling 250 to play multiway OOP is negative EV. Preserve chips for better spots — fold.

9-2 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker, commonly known as "the worst hand." While I'm getting 3.25:1 pot odds (needing to call 200 into a 450 pot), I'm significantly out of position against Flash's button raise. Even accounting for positional odds and blind defense, this hand is essentially unplayable. Flash has shown reasonable aggression throughout the session, and playing marginal hands out of position is a losing strategy. Folding preserves my stack for premium situations where I have a real edge. With 23,900 chips and still 67 hands remaining, I can afford to be selective and wait for better opportunities.

3-handed with me on the button and 29 BB effective — 4♦7♦ is a speculative but playable hand in position. With only the blinds in and both opponents showing wide/open tendencies, I want to seize initiative, avoid a cheap multi-way flop, and use my positional advantage. A standard open to 300 (≈3x the big blind / consistent with table) gives fold equity and keeps weaker ranges in. Plan: C-bet favorable low/dry flops, play cautiously if I face significant resistance postflop. If I face a large 3-bet (big portion of my stack) I’ll fold; I may entertain calling very small 3-bets for pot odds and position.

Minni is critically short-stacked (2,605 chips, ~9BB), making this an ideal isolation opportunity. I raise to 550 total to apply pressure and likely eliminate him from the pot or force a showdown where J9o has decent equity against his desperation range. This also puts Flash in a difficult spot with a call/fold decision. The min-raise is aggressive but calculated - it wins the pot a significant percentage of the time and sets up favorable postflop play if called.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5s. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) made a very small 3-bet to 550. Although the price to call is relatively cheap (450 more into a pot of 950), playing a speculative, gapped-suited hand out of position against two opponents—one of whom has been very active recently—is not ideal. Claude's small 3-bet could be a trap or a light squeeze, but 7s 5s is too weak to continue with, especially since Minni might jam her remaining short stack (2305 more). I will fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

Call (total 550). I’m on the button with 4♦7♦ suited and only ~26–29 BB effective — this is a speculative but playable hand in position. Claude’s 3‑bet to 550 is very small and likely contains a lot of wide hands; calling 250 more into a ~950 pot gives ~17% equity needed (good pot odds) and lets me use position to realize equity and play postflop. 4‑betting with 4♦7♦ is too ambitious here (would bloat the pot and risk a shove), and folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: see the flop and play aggressively on favorable low/diamond flops (backdoor/straight/flush potential), pot‑control or check-fold on high Broadway/overcard boards, and fold to large shove/very strong action.

Checking is the optimal play with J9o on a completely whiffed flop (2d-6c-Qs). My hand is too weak to bet with, and checking allows Minni's short stack to define the action. If he shoves, I'll have acceptable pot odds (roughly 2.4:1) with my gutshot draw and unpaired cards that could improve. If he checks, I get to see more cards cheaply. This prevents building the pot with a marginal holding while keeping the flexibility to call a shove if the odds are right.

I’m on the button in position after calling Claude’s small preflop 3‑bet. The flop 2♦‑6♣‑Q♠ is relatively dry and misses a large portion of his 3‑betting range; his check looks like weakness or pot‑control. My 4♦7♦ has no pair but a tiny backdoor diamond draw and some showdown/runner outs — not enough to slow‑play. I want to seize initiative and use positional fold equity, so I’ll make a standard half‑pot C‑bet (600 into 1200). This size pressures his missed hands while leaving me room to fold to a strong check‑raise or shove, and it keeps the pot manageable if he calls — I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously versus big resistance.

Call 600 with J9o. Against Minni's desperation range with <2K chips, I have roughly 35-40% equity with a gutshot and overcard potential. The pot odds of 4:1 justify the call easily. This is a profitable spot to apply chip pressure to a short-stacked opponent in early tournament stages.

Betting 1000 on the turn with J9o and an open-ended straight draw. Minni checked, showing weakness, and is extremely short (1,755 chips). My OESD combined with overcard equity gives me solid equity against his desperation range. The bet applies maximum pressure: either he folds weak hands or commits with worse holdings. This is a value bet that extracts the most from his short stack in a high-leverage spot.

Claude 3‑bet preflop, check‑called my flop c‑bet and now barrels the turn Ace for 1000 into a 3400 pot. That line is heavily weighted to value (A‑x that just improved, two‑pair, sets or strong Qx/pocket pair protection). I have 4♦7♦ with no pair and the backdoor diamond flush is dead after the club turn. Calling 1000 requires roughly 18.5% equity (1000 to win ~5400) but my only clean outs are three 4s and three 7s (~13% raw) and many of those will still lose to his value hands. Shoving as a bluff is too thin against his line and big stack. Therefore this is a clear fold — preserve my ~17.5BB for a better spot rather than calling or risking an all‑in with almost no equity.

Raise to 300 from the button with A4o. Minni is critically short-stacked at 1,655 chips (16.5 BB) and will be forced to make decisions quickly. A standard 3x raise applies pressure, wins the pot if both opponents fold, and puts me in a strong position if Minni shoves. My button position and stack size advantage are significant. While A4o is marginal, it's strong enough to build pots against a desperate opponent in a late tournament stage (hand 85/150).

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o). Claude has opened to 300 from the Button. While I am getting decent odds to call, T2o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position, and with Minni sitting on a very short stack in the Big Blind, there is a high risk of being squeezed or facing a shove. Given the tournament context and the weakness of my hand, folding is the standard and most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Claude opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the BB with J♣8♣ and a 1,655 stack (~16.6bb). J8s is marginal but has good postflop playability vs a single raise, and the pot odds justify defending: I must call 200 more to win the 450 pot (need ~30.8% equity). Shoving is too loose here (I’d be risking a lot for limited fold equity), and a 3-bet OOP with this hand is suboptimal. Folding is too tight. I will call (put 200 more, 300 total) and plan to play the flop conservatively — continue aggressively if I hit pair/flush/strong draw, fold to significant pressure on dry, missed flops.

I defended J♣8♣ preflop because of pot odds and post‑flop playability. The flop K♣‑Q♦‑7♠ misses me: I have no pair, only 6 outs to pair one of my cards (≈24% to hit by the river) and only a ≈4% chance for a runner‑runner club flush. With a short stack (~14.5bb) and a 650 pot, I want to control the size of the pot and not barrel into aggression with air. I’ll check and see Claude’s reaction — plan is to fold to a sizable c‑bet or any raise, but to call a small c‑bet (~≤1/3 pot) to attempt a float and see a turn. If he checks back I get a free turn to try to realize equity or take the pot later.

Checking with A4o on a Q-K-7 board. While I have an Ace, this is a weak kicker in a tough spot. The high board connects with many hands Minni could hold (pairs, AQ, AK, etc.). Checking allows me to control pot size with a marginal hand, gather information about Minni's strength before committing chips, and play a safer line. Given my chip advantage and Minni's desperate short stack situation, I can see another card cheaply. If Minni shoves, I can make an informed decision; if he checks, I can continue on the turn with better board runout knowledge.

I defended preflop with J♣8♣ and checked the flop to control pot size. On the turn (K♣ Q♦ 7♠ T♦) I still have no pair but I do have 10 clean outs to improve on the river (3 Jacks + 3 Eights + 4 Nines) → ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to make at least a pair/straight. The pot is 650 and my stack is 1,455 — a turn bet would bloat the pot and commit a large portion of my short stack with only ~22% equity. Claude’s check‑check line looks like a fairly controlled/medium range, and a small bet is unlikely to fold enough of his range to make aggression +EV. If I check I get a free river and can call a small river bet; break‑even call size on the river (with ~21.7% equity into a 650 pot) is about 180 chips, so I’ll call modest river bets but fold large ones. Plan: see the river for free, only invest more if I improve or face a very small bet on the river.

Checking on the turn with A4o against critically short-stacked Minni (1,455 chips). The high board (Q-K-7-T) doesn't help my marginal hand. My 24,850 stack gives me tremendous positional advantage - I can see the river free and let Minni face the final decision with his desperation stack. Betting here risks chips with a weak hand against a range that either folds (winning small pot) or shoves (forcing a marginal call). By checking, I either win at showdown if my ace holds or can fold to a river shove without additional commitment. This conservative line is optimal given my chip advantage and hand weakness.

I rivered second pair (8♣ with J♣ kicker). Pot is 650 and my stack is ~1,455 while Claude is very deep. Claude opened preflop then passively checked flop and turn — that line contains many hands that beat me (Kx/Qx/Tx, possibly sets or straights) and some weaker A‑high/missed holdings. A small value bet would get called by many hands that beat me and only fold the very weakest parts of his range, so there’s little fold equity and I risk building a pot where I’m behind. With mediocre showdown value but no strong reason to bluff, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and go to showdown rather than invest more chips. Action: check (amount 0).

River check for showdown with As4d. Minni has checked through the entire hand, indicating significant weakness. My Ace-high is likely the best hand against his range. With my massive chip advantage (24,850 vs 1,455) and strong positional control throughout, going to showdown is optimal. Betting offers no value against a range that either folds weak hands (wasting the pot) or calls with something better. Checking secures the pot at showdown where my Ace-high should prevail against Minni's checked-through range on a K-Q-7-T-8 board.

I am on the button in a three-handed game holding Qs 9c. This is a standard opening hand in this position. Minni in the Small Blind is very short-stacked with just over 20 Big Blinds (2055 chips), while Claude is the chip leader. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot. If Minni shoves, I will have to evaluate based on the pot odds, but for now, an opening raise is the most sound strategic move.

I have 7♦8♠ in the small blind with a 2,055-chip stack (~20.5 BB) facing a button open to 300. This is a marginal, connected but offsuit hand that plays reasonably heads-up but poorly multiway. The big blind still has to act — a small squeeze to 500 is unlikely to fold many button opens and just puts chips OOP; shoving ~20 BB is too much risk with a hand that is easily dominated. I’ll take a conservative but constructive line: call the 250 (300 total committed) to see a flop, preserve stack flexibility, and re-evaluate based on how the big blind and button act and on the flop texture. If I face a large 3-bet or heavy post‑flop pressure without a good flop, I will fold.

Raising with pocket tens in the big blind after Flash's button raise and Minni's SB call. Strong hand warrants aggression. Sizing to 900 builds the pot, applies pressure to Minni's critical short stack (1,805), and gets value from Flash's likely wide range. This is a premium hand that should play for value in position.

I opened to 300 with Q9o from the button, which is a standard play in a three-handed game. However, Claude has responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. While I do have position on Claude for the rest of the hand, Q9o is a relatively weak hand to defend against a 3-bet, especially with a short-stacked player like Minni still to act behind Claude's raise (though she originally just called my open). Claude has been playing very aggressively throughout the tournament, but I have a healthy stack and there's no reason to commit more chips with a dominated or marginal hand in this spot. I'll wait for a better opportunity to take on the chip leader.

I’m in the small blind with 7♦8♠ and a short ~18 BB stack (1805). Button opened to 300 and I called; Claude (BB) then 3‑bet to 900 and the button folded. Pot is ~1500 and I must call 600 more. Facing a big, deep‑stack 3‑bet from Claude (who has shown aggression and big sizing in prior hands), my offsuit connector is either dominated or has poor equity vs his likely value-heavy 3‑bet range. The pot odds (≈1500:600 → ~2.5:1) require ~28.6% equity; out of position postflop with a marginal hand and limited implied odds, that’s not acceptable. Shoving or min‑raising would commit most of my stack into a player who can easily call and is likely ahead. I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Hand is 4♠9♣ on the button — a very weak offsuit hand. The decision is between folding, calling 100 into a 150 pot (you’d need ~40% equity) or raising to the minimum (200) / shoving (~18 BB stack). My stack (~1,805 ≈ 18 BB) is short-ish but not so short that I should shove marginal offsuit junk, and both opponents (Claude and Flash) have shown willingness to call big pots and make big bets. A call or small raise will often put me in multi-way or heads-up spots dominated postflop; a shove will get called by better hands too often. Preserve chips, wait for a real shove or a stronger holding — fold here.

In this heads-up situation against Flash (Minni folded from button), I'm in the small blind with 8c-7c. While this is a weak hand in absolute terms, several factors make calling correct:

1. **Pot Odds**: I'm getting 3:1 odds (150 in pot, 50 to call), which is favorable for a wide range.

2. **Position**: In heads-up play, the small blind has position postflop, which is valuable.

3. **Hand Quality in Heads-Up**: 8-7 of the same suit has decent playability in heads-up. It has straight and flush potential, and can win unimproved.

4. **Stack Dynamics**: My 25,400 stack is significantly larger than both opponents, giving me flexibility. Flash has 12,645 and Minni has only 1,805 remaining.

5. **Short-Handed Strategy**: In short-handed games, I should be playing wider ranges from early positions, especially with reasonable drawing hands.

6. **Cost**: Only 50 chips to call - minimal risk relative to my stack.

The hand isn't strong enough to raise profitably (Flash would likely fold most weaker holdings from BB), but it's definitely worth seeing a flop against one opponent with reasonable pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Minni) has folded, and the Small Blind (Claude) has just completed the blind. J6o is a weak hand, and while it might be ahead of Claude's limping range, there is no significant value in raising here to build a pot out of position against the chip leader. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and keeps the pot small, which is ideal for a hand of this strength.

Betting with top pair (8s) on a relatively dry flop. This is a standard value bet that builds the pot, protects against draw outs, and extracts value from Flash's wide BB range. The bet size of 120 (60% of the 200 pot) is appropriately sized to apply pressure while allowing Flash to continue with draws or worse made hands. My stack advantage allows me to play aggressively and control the hand.

In this limped pot, the flop of 8-5-2 is very dry. I hold Jack-high (J6o) with no real draw other than a very weak backdoor straight. Claude, the chip leader, has led out with a bet of 120 into a 200-chip pot. While Jack-high might occasionally be the best hand here against a wide range, it has very little equity to improve and is difficult to play on later streets against an aggressive big stack. Since I have no piece of this board and no reliable way to continue if the turn is an overcard or if Claude continues his aggression, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

With pocket Kings on the button facing a critically short-stacked big blind (Minni with only 1,705 chips) and a moderate stack in the small blind (Flash with 12,595), I'm raising to 300 (3x BB). This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively. The raise builds the pot, puts pressure on both opponents, and gives me a chance to either win immediately or get into a favorable situation if called. Given Minni's desperation stack, she may be forced to commit, which is ideal given my hand strength.

I am holding Jack of Hearts and Deuce of Spades in the Small Blind. The Button (Claude) has raised to 300. My hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position, especially against a large stack like Claude's. Additionally, Minni in the Big Blind is very short-stacked and may choose to shove over the raise, which would force me to fold anyway. There is no reason to invest chips with such a low-equity hand.

Call 300. 7♦9♦s is a playable defence from the big blind vs a button open — good post‑flop playability (straights/flushes) and reasonable equity against a wide BTN opening range. The immediate cost is 200 more into a 450 pot (≈30–33% break‑even equity), so the pot odds are acceptable. With only ~17 BB I don’t want to jam a marginal hand and face a call from a very deep opponent; calling preserves chips, lets me realize equity and apply pot‑control postflop. Plan: see the flop, continue with strong connects or draws, fold to large, committed aggression when missed.

Board is 8h-3c-3h and Claude (preflop raiser) checked to me. I hold 9♦7♦ — no pair, only 6 clean outs (three 7s + three 9s) — ~12.8% to hit on the turn, ~24% to hit by the river. With only ~15 BB effective I don’t want to bloat the pot or commit with a marginal hand when a raise would put me in a very tough spot. Claude checking shows weakness (or a slowplay), so pot-control and giving myself a free card is the best line: check and fold to clear, heavy aggression unless I improve. If Claude bets a small amount I can call with my redraw equity; if he fires big I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

Continuation bet with premium pocket Kings on a relatively safe flop. Minni is critically short (1505 chips), making her fold/call decision high-pressure. The 8-3-3 board doesn't threaten my hand strength. A 500 bet applies sufficient pressure to extract value or win immediately while respecting her short-stack range.

Board: 8♥-3♣-3♥. I hold 9♦7♦ — no pair, only 6 clean outs (three 7s, three 9s). Pot is 1,150 and Claude bets 500. If I call 500 the pot becomes 1,650, so my break-even equity is 500/1,650 ≈ 30.3%. With 6 outs my chance to improve by the river is ≈24% (≈12.8% to hit the turn). Claude was the preflop raiser and has now fired a large ~77% pot c-bet, a sizing that polarizes toward value here; against his likely continuation-range (8x, overpairs, many pocket pairs, some bluffs) my raw equity is below the break‑even point. With only ~15 BB I can’t risk a third (or more) of my stack on a marginal draw that often loses or is dominated, and raising/shoving would be high variance and will frequently be called by better hands. Best line is to preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Holding Ace-Five suited on the button in a three-handed game is a strong position. I have a stack of over 125 big blinds, while the small blind (Minni) is quite short-stacked at approximately 14.5 big blinds. I want to put pressure on the blinds and potentially isolate Minni or win the pot immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to maintain aggression and leverage my position.

I’m short-stacked (1,455 chips ≈ 14.5bb) in the small blind facing a button open to 300. A2o in a three-handed spot with ~15bb is a standard shove/fold spot — calling 250 leaves me OOP with a marginal hand and a lot of decisions left, and a small reraise (e.g. to 500) gives much less fold equity and invites the big blind to isolate. Shoving maximizes fold equity vs the button’s wide opening range and simplifies the decision. If called I still have reasonable equity with an ace. I’ll go all-in to apply maximum pressure and either steal the pot or take a flip with decent equity.

Calling the 3-bet with A6o is justified by pot odds of approximately 1.37:1. Against Minni's 3-betting range and Flash's continued presence, A6o has sufficient equity (~42-45%) to make this call profitable. The Ace provides good playability, especially if it hits top pair. With 25,800 chips, I'm deep enough to navigate post-flop play intelligently despite being out of position. The hand has reasonable value and I can extract information from my opponents' actions.

I started the action with a raise to 300 with Ah 5h, a strong suited Ace on the button. Minni, who is extremely short-stacked, has effectively gone all-in by raising to 1455 (leaving only 50 chips). Claude, the chip leader, called this raise from the Big Blind. I need to call 1155 more to stay in the hand. The pot odds are very favorable (calling 1155 to win a pot that will be over 4300). Folding is not an option. While I could raise to isolate, Claude has shown a willingness to see flops and has a massive stack; calling allows me to see the flop in position and keep the pot manageable against the big stack while still having great equity to beat Minni's likely wide all-in range.

I'm already all-in from preflop (shoved ~1,455, leaving 50). Both opponents called, so my stack is committed and there are no additional chips I can put in. The flop Ks 8h 5d misses my As2c; I have 6 clean outs (three Aces + three deuces) — roughly ~24% to hit by the river (rule-of-4), somewhat less multiway and vs likely Kx/pair-heavy calling ranges. There is nothing for me to do strategically other than go to showdown, so I check (0).

Checking is the prudent play with A6o on a K-8-5 board. I have ace-high with no development and am out of position to Flash. Minni is nearly all-in with 50 chips remaining. By checking, I let Flash make his decision, control pot size with a marginal holding, and keep options open for the turn. Betting ace-high would be speculative into a 3-bet pot where Flash likely has reasonable equity already.

On this flop of Ks 8h 5d, I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker. Pre-flop, Minni essentially went all-in (leaving only 50 chips), and Claude called the large raise. With Claude checking to me, and considering he is the chip leader who can afford to play a wide range, I prefer to check back. My pair of fives has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any King or Eight in Claude's range. Checking allows me to control the pot size against the big stack while still being in a good position to see the turn and potentially win against Minni's short-stack range.

I shoved all-in preflop for ~1,455 and am effectively pot‑committed with a remaining stack of 50 — both opponents called. On the turn (Ks 8h 5d Qc) my A2o has 6 clean outs (~22–24% to improve by the river), but I have no more chips to put in. To Call is 0, so the only sensible legal action is to check and take it to showdown.

Checking the turn with A6o on a K-8-5-Q board. I have ace-high with no improvement and am out of position to Flash's 11140 stack. Minni is committed with 50 chips. Betting ace-high into a 3-bet caller is speculative and risks getting check-raised into a tough spot. Checking allows me to see if Flash bets and gather information before the river while controlling pot size with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the Queen of Clubs is another overcard to my pair of fives. Since Minni is essentially all-in with only 50 chips remaining and Claude has checked for the second time, there is no reason to bet into a side pot. Claude's passive play could indicate a marginal hand or a trap, but given his history of being sticky, a bluff is unlikely to succeed. I will check to see the river for free and attempt to realize my equity against Minni's range while keeping the pot size controlled against the chip leader.

I shoved preflop and am effectively at showdown with A♠2♣ on K♠8♥5♦Q♣K♦. I missed my six outs and have no improvement on the river — I’m left with Ace‑high. This is a three‑way pot against ranges that include Kx, Qx, pairs and full houses. A tiny bet of 50 into a 4,365 pot has almost no fold equity versus two callers and simply risks my remaining chips for no realistic gain. Checking to showdown preserves my chips and is the least costly, most rational line here.

Checking on the river with A-high. All three players checked flop and turn, and Minni just checked the river, indicating weakness across the board. My ace-high has minimal value but is worth a showdown. Betting would be speculative with no credible story - I'd likely be called by Flash and lose, or get check-raised. The risk/reward doesn't justify a wager here.

On this board of Ks-8h-5d-Qc-Kd, my best five-card hand is two pair, Kings and Fives with an Ace kicker (Ks-Kd-5d-5h-Ah). However, notice that with the Queen and Eight on the board, my pair of fives is essentially irrelevant unless no one else has a pair higher than a five. More accurately, my hand is Kings (from the board) with an Ace, Queen, and Eight as kickers (K-K-A-Q-8). If Claude or Minni has any King, they have trips. If they have a Queen, they have two pair (KK and QQ). If they have an 8, they have two pair (KK and 88). Given the pre-flop action where Minni 3-bet and Claude called a large amount, it is very possible someone holds a King, Queen, or an Ace. Claude has checked every street, which could be a weak King or a Queen, or simply a missed hand. Since I have showdown value with Ace-high on this paired board, but am unlikely to get called by worse or fold out a better hand (like a Queen), checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown.

Stack is tiny (50), call 50 is effectively an all-in. Pot is 150 so calling 50 builds a 200 pot — break-even equity is only 25%. J♣9♠ has reasonable showdown equity vs random hands and I have position, so this is a shove-or-fold spot where I need to take a shot to double up. Folding leaves me blinded out with no realistic path to survival. I’ll commit the 50 to try to double up.

With 6s5s from small blind against a button caller, I have a weak hand in poor position. Minni's passive call suggests weakness. Checking completes my call and lets Flash act, preserving chips while keeping position flexible. The hand doesn't warrant aggression out of position with my large 24,395 stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 8-7 offsuit. The Button (Minni) appears to be all-in for a small amount (50 chips), and the Small Blind (Claude) has called the bet. Since I have already contributed the 100-chip big blind and there is no additional amount to call, I will check to see the flop for free. This allows me to realize my equity without risking more chips with a marginal starting hand against a large stack like Claude.

Pair of 6s on a 2d 2h 6d flop is a decent hand but not strong enough to get aggressive out of position in a 3-way pot. Checking controls the pot size, lets me see opponent action, and preserves my flexibility. My flush draw gives me additional equity if I need to call a bet. The weak pre-flop action suggests opponents have marginal hands, so slow-playing is sound.

The button (Minni) is all-in, and the small blind (Claude) has checked to me on a flop of 2-2-6. In tournament play, when an opponent is all-in, it is often optimal for the remaining active players to check the hand down to maximize the probability of eliminating the short-stacked player. Since I only have 8-high and there is no reason to build a side pot against a much larger stack like Claude's, I will check and see the turn for free.

Betting the turn to apply pressure with my pair of 6s and strong flush draw. Everyone checked the flop, indicating weakness. Heads-up against Flash now with my stack advantage (24,345 vs 15,405), I should take the initiative. My nine-card flush draw gives me excellent equity even if behind. A bet of 300 is reasonable relative to the 250 pot and applies meaningful pressure to win immediately or build a pot where I have good equity with my draw and current pair.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8-7 offsuit. On a board of 2-2-6-9, my hand has zero showdown value and no real draw. Claude (Small Blind) has bet 300 into the side pot. While it is generally beneficial to check the hand down to eliminate the all-in player (Minni), Claude's bet indicates he either has a piece of the board or is attempting to isolate the main pot by pushing me out. Given that I have missed the board entirely and have no equity to justify a call, folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack.

3-5 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. Despite having position on the button, this hand has virtually no equity. Minni is critically short with only 50 chips in the pot, and Flash has a healthy 15k stack. Even in position, playing trash hands from the button with 90 BBs in my stack is poor strategy. The risk-reward doesn't justify entering the pot with 3-5. I'll fold and wait for better opportunities.

Claude has folded his button. Minni, in the big blind, only has 50 chips remaining, which means she is already all-in for half of the big blind amount. I have already posted 50 chips as the small blind and only need to call 50 more to see the showdown. With 94o, I have roughly 35-40% equity against a random hand. Given the pot odds (calling 50 to win a total pot of 150), I only need 33% equity to make this a profitable call. Since Minni is already all-in, there is no risk of being outplayed on later streets, making this a simple mathematical call to attempt to eliminate a short-stacked opponent.

I’m effectively crippled — I have only 50 chips left (less than a big blind). With the SB having just checked/called and no raise, the minimum legal raise is 150 total, which is exactly my remaining stack if I shove. With a sub‑1BB stack the correct strategy is push/fold: shove virtually any two cards to maximize fold equity and avoid being blinded out. 7♥T♦ is fine heads‑up — it has reasonable showdown equity if called and decent chance the SB folds to an all‑in. Folding or passively checking surrenders my only realistic chance to double up. I’ll shove all‑in by raising to 150 total (100 already posted + 50 more).

Minni is all-in for her remaining 150 chips. I have already committed 100 chips to the pot and only need to call an additional 50 chips to see a showdown. With a total pot of 250 chips, I am getting 4-to-1 odds (50 to call, 200 already in the pot plus my 50). This requires only 20% equity to be a profitable call. Even with a weak hand like 9-4 offsuit, I have sufficient equity against a random range or any short-stack shove. Additionally, calling gives me a chance to eliminate a player from the tournament.

Holding Ace-Queen offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation is a premium hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand, as this hand dominates a large portion of the Big Blind's defending range.

7-5 offsuit is a marginal weak hand. While we're in heads-up play where hand ranges are wider, this is still below the threshold for calling a raise.
